summary: Charity Majors reflects on her journey from being an open-source zealot who dismissed software vendors to becoming one herself, and the karmic experience of having her expertise discounted because she's now 'selling something.' She offers practical advice for vendor-side engineers to maintain credibility by leading with bias disclosure, engaging competitors' strongest arguments, and being generous with expertise.
key_points: Open source culture breeds an often unexamined contempt for software vendors that doesn't hold up under scrutiny
Most production-caliber code is written by people who were paid to write it, not volunteers
Good software has tremendous value, and the people who build it deserve to recoup a slice of that value
Sales is about repeated games and relationships, not one-time tricks
Vendors can maintain credibility by disclosing bias, engaging competitors' best arguments, and being their own biggest critic
money_quotes: I built something because I cared.
The overwhelming majority of production-caliber code is written by somebody who was paid to write it — not by volunteers.

Good software has tremendous value. It unlocks value and creates value, it pays enormous ongoing dividends in dollars and productivity, and the people who build it, support it, and bring it to market fully deserve to recoup a slice of the value they created for others.
It's a shitty, self-limiting stance to take as a default. We are all here to build things, not tear things down.
Never do a deal unless you'd do both sides of the deal.
key_insight: The open-source world's reflexive contempt for software vendors is a self-limiting bias that ignores how much value paid software creates, and vendor-side engineers can maintain credibility by leading with transparency about their biases rather than hiding them.
